title: Installing Global Tools
intro: Some tools need to be available everywhere on your computer, not just in one project. Global installs and npx give you two ways to use CLI tools -- permanently installed or run once on demand. Let's learn when to use each approach and practice both.

teach.title: Global Install = Installing an App on Your Phone
teach.body: When you install an app on your phone, it's available everywhere -- from the home screen, from any folder, anytime. **npm install -g** works the same way. It installs a tool on your entire computer, not just one project. After `npm install -g vercel`, you can type `vercel` in any terminal window, in any folder, and it works. It's permanently on your machine, like an app on your phone.

teach.title: Local Install = Buying a Tool for a Specific Workshop
teach.body: Imagine you have two workshops -- one for woodworking and one for electronics. You buy a soldering iron only for the electronics workshop. It stays there; you don't carry it to the wood shop. **Local installs** (regular `npm install`) work the same way. The package only exists inside that project's node_modules folder. Other projects can't see it. This keeps projects isolated -- Project A might need version 2 of a tool while Project B needs version 3.

teach.title: Global vs Local: When to Use Each
teach.body:
**Install globally (-g)** when:
- The tool is a standalone CLI you run from anywhere (vercel, netlify, create-react-app)
- You use it across multiple projects
- It's a development utility, not a project dependency

**Install locally** (default) when:
- The package is code your project imports (react, express, axios)
- Different projects might need different versions
- You want the project to be portable (anyone who clones it gets the right version)

teach.title: npx: Run Without Installing
teach.body:
**npx** lets you run a package command without installing it permanently. It downloads the package, runs it once, and throws it away.

- `npx create-next-app my-site` -- Runs create-next-app without installing it globally
- `npx eslint .` -- Runs ESLint without adding it to your project

npx is perfect for one-time commands or tools you don't use often. No clutter, no outdated global installs.

teach.title: Global Install vs npx
teach.code:
# Global install: permanently available
npm install -g vercel
vercel --version          # Works from anywhere

# npx: run once without installing
npx create-next-app my-app   # Downloads, runs, done
npx cowsay "Hello World"      # Fun one-time command
teach.annotation: Global installs stay on your machine until you uninstall them. npx downloads, runs the command, and cleans up. Use npx for project generators and one-off tools.

teach.body: Knowing when to install globally vs locally vs using npx is a skill that separates beginners from confident developers. When a client says 'deploy this to Vercel' or 'scaffold a new Next.js project,' you execute the right command in seconds. That speed and confidence is what vibecoding clients pay premium rates for.

Use npm install -g for CLI tools you need everywhere, regular npm install for project-specific packages, and npx for one-time commands. Global tools are like phone apps (available everywhere), local installs are like workshop tools (stay in one project), and npx is like borrowing a tool for a quick job.

steps.instruction: Install the Vercel CLI globally so you can deploy projects from any folder.
steps.hint: Use 'npm install -g' followed by the package name. The -g flag makes it available everywhere.

steps.instruction: Verify that vercel was installed globally by checking its version.
steps.hint: Run the tool name followed by '--version' to confirm it's installed and see which version.

steps.instruction: Now use npx to create a new Next.js app called 'my-site' without installing create-next-app globally.
steps.output:
Creating a new Next.js app in ~/my-site.

Using npm.

Installing dependencies:
- react
- react-dom
- next

Success! Created my-site at ~/my-site
steps.hint: Use 'npx' followed by the package command. npx downloads and runs it without a permanent install.

steps.instruction: Check which packages are installed globally on your system to see vercel in the list.
steps.hint: Use 'npm list -g --depth=0' to see all globally installed packages without showing their sub-dependencies.

exercise.completionMessage: You now know three ways to use npm packages: global installs for CLI tools you use everywhere, local installs for project dependencies, and npx for one-time commands. Notice how create-next-app doesn't appear in your global list -- npx ran it and cleaned up. Vercel is there because you installed it globally.
